request.question: Are Solo and La Croix Sparkling Water produced in the same country ?

title: Col de la Croix Fry
Highland Spring
Solo (Norwegian soft drink)
La Croix Fry
Lac La Croix Water Aerodrome
La Croix Sparkling Water
Alain Hertoghe
Les Sept Paroles du Christ sur la Croix
Col du Glandon
La Croix
sentences: The Col de la Croix Fry (1467 m ) is a mountain pass located in the Chaîne des Aravis, between Manigod and La Clusaz in the Haute-Savoie department of France.
The road over the col is used occasionally by the Tour de France cycle race with the tour crossing the pass on Stage 19 of the 2013 Tour.
At the summit is the village of La Croix Fry.
Highland Spring is a Scottish supplier of bottled water.
It produces still and sparkling water at its factory in Blackford, Perth and Kinross, although despite the name this area is not actually within the Scottish Highlands.
Highland Spring was the highest-selling sparkling water in the UK in 2008 and consolidated its first place position in the UK still water market.
Solo is an orange-flavoured soft drink, owned by the Norwegian companies Ringnes, Oskar Sylte, Aass, and Mack.
The recipe was originally Spanish, and brought to the Tønsberg Bryggeri by Torleif Gulliksrud in 1934.
Solo quickly became Norway's most popular soft drink, and until the 1960s was bigger than Coca-Cola in Norway.
In 1999, Pepsi passed Solo in market share, leaving Solo as third most popular.
La Croix Fry is a small village contained within the Domaine de Manigod.
With a population of under 600 people La Croix Fry is a popular site for hill walking on the Plateau de Beauregard as well as skiing in the winter.
Lac La Croix Water Aerodrome, (TC LID: CJU9) , is located on Lac La Croix, Ontario, Canada.

LaCroix or La Croix ( ) is a soda water distributed by the Sundance Beverage Company, a subsidiary of National Beverage Corp. Originating from G. Heileman Brewing Company in La Crosse, Wisconsin, LaCroix was first produced in 1981.
In the United States, often appealing to sparkling water consumers looking for an alternative to an import, LaCroix markets itself as an "innocent" and "all occasion" beverage.
Alain Hertoghe (born 1959) is a Belgian journalist, formerly an employee of the French Catholic newspaper "La Croix".
He was fired in December 2003 after writing a book critical of the coverage of the U.S. invasion of Iraq by French newspapers "Le Monde", "Le Figaro", "Libération", "Ouest-France" and "La Croix".

Les Sept Paroles du Christ sur la Croix (composed 1859) is a musical setting of The Seven Last Words of Christ by César Franck, though the name in French often refers to an equally well or better known homonymous work by Charles Gounod "Les sept paroles de N.S. Jesus-Christ sur la croix".
Col du Glandon (1924 m ) is a high mountain pass in the Dauphiné Alps in Savoie, France, linking Le Bourg-d'Oisans to La Chambre.
It is situated between the Belledonne, Grandes Rousses and Arvan-Villards mountain ranges, west of the Col de la Croix de Fer.
The road over the Col du Glandon was opened in 1898, although it was not linked to the Col de la Croix de Fer until 1912.
La Croix (] ; English: "The Cross") is a daily French general-interest Roman Catholic newspaper.
It is published in Paris and distributed throughout France, with a circulation of just under 110,000 as of 2009.
It is not explicitly left or right on major political issues, rather adopting the Church's position.
However, "La Croix" ought not be confused with a religious newspaper—its topics are of general interest: world news, the economy, religion and spirituality, parenting, culture and science.
It vigorously advocated for traditional Catholicism while at the same time innovating with the most modern technology and distribution systems.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Are Solo and La Croix Sparkling Water produced in the same country ?
hop1_docs: La Croix Sparkling Water | LaCroix or La Croix ( ) is a soda water distributed by the Sundance Beverage Company, a subsidiary of National Beverage Corp. Originating from G. Heileman Brewing Company in La Crosse, Wisconsin, LaCroix was first produced in 1981.  In the United States, often appealing to sparkling water consumers looking for an alternative to an import, LaCroix markets itself as an "innocent" and "all occasion" beverage.
Lac La Croix Water Aerodrome | Lac La Croix Water Aerodrome, (TC LID: CJU9) , is located on Lac La Croix, Ontario, Canada.

Highland Spring | Highland Spring is a Scottish supplier of bottled water.  It produces still and sparkling water at its factory in Blackford, Perth and Kinross, although despite the name this area is not actually within the Scottish Highlands.  Highland Spring was the highest-selling sparkling water in the UK in 2008 and consolidated its first place position in the UK still water market.
Tanja La Croix | Tanja La Croix (born as Tanja Wettach on April 8, 1982) is a House DJ and Producer from Switzerland.

Cava (Spanish wine) | Cava (] , plural "caves") is a sparkling wine of "Denominación de Origen" (DO) status from Catalonia, also produced in other regions of Spain.  It may be white ("blanc") or rosé ("rosat").  The macabeu, parellada and xarel·lo are the most popular and traditional grape varieties for producing cava.  Only wines produced in the champenoise traditional method may be labelled "cava"; those produced by other processes may only be called "sparkling wines" ("vinos espumosos").  About 95% of all cava is produced in the Penedès area in Catalonia, with the village of Sant Sadurní d'Anoia being home to many of Spain's largest production houses.  The two major producers are Codorníu and Freixenet.  Cava is also produced in other villages in Aragon, Castile and León, Extremadura, La Rioja, Basque Country, Navarre and Valencia.

Coteaux Champenois AOC | Coteaux Champenois is a wine Appellation d'Origine Contrôlée (AOC) in the Champagne province of France.  It covers the same area as sparkling Champagne production, but covers only still wines.  The grapes are the same as those allowed for sparkling Champagne: Pinot noir and Pinot Meunier for red wines, and Chardonnay for whites.  Rosé wines are made very rarely, but there is also a rosé AOC in the Champagne region, Rosé des Riceys.  Like sparkling Champagne, most wines are non-vintage.  Production is small, especially in vintages where yields are low, given the high demand for Champagne and the higher profit of producing sparkling wine.

La Croix | La Croix (] ; English: "The Cross") is a daily French general-interest Roman Catholic newspaper.  It is published in Paris and distributed throughout France, with a circulation of just under 110,000 as of 2009.  It is not explicitly left or right on major political issues, rather adopting the Church's position.  However, "La Croix" ought not be confused with a religious newspaper—its topics are of general interest: world news, the economy, religion and spirituality, parenting, culture and science.  It vigorously advocated for traditional Catholicism while at the same time innovating with the most modern technology and distribution systems.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - La Croix Sparkling Water: passage [1] ("Originating from G. Heileman Brewing Company in La Crosse, Wisconsin") and ("first produced in 1981") — cite: La Croix Sparkling Water (passage [1]).
  - Solo: no matching passage found in the provided set (no passage mentions "Solo" as a beverage).
- Ambiguities:
  - "La Croix" appears twice in passages: "La Croix Sparkling Water" (passage [1]) and "La Croix" newspaper (passage [7]) — two distinct candidate titles. 
  - "Solo" is missing entirely from the passages; possible multiple "Solo" beverage pages are not listed here (disambiguation needed).
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entities in the current passages: "La Croix Sparkling Water" (present) and the missing target "Solo" (not present).
  - Precise missing fact needed: Solo's country of production (the country where Solo is produced). Also confirm that La Croix's production country corresponds to its origin (La Crosse, Wisconsin → United States) by checking lede/infobox.
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Fetch "Solo (soft drink)" or "Solo (beverage)" page (or "Solo (disambiguation)") — check the lede/infobox and sections titled "Production", "Manufacturer", or "Country of origin" for the production country. Example search: "Solo soft drink country of origin" or open "Solo (soft drink)".
  2) Re-open "La Croix Sparkling Water" page (passage [1]) and verify lede/infobox and "History"/"Production" sections to confirm production country (look for "La Crosse, Wisconsin", "United States", or manufacturer/distributor details).
  3) If Solo has multiple national variants, fetch each candidate page (e.g., "Solo (Australia)", "Solo (Norway)" etc.) from a disambiguation page and check their production-country lines.
Final note: I will not answer whether they are produced in the same country until the Solo page(s) and the specified sections of the La Croix page are retrieved.
response.hop2_query: Solo soft drink country of origin
hop2_docs: Solo (Norwegian soft drink) | Solo is an orange-flavoured soft drink, owned by the Norwegian companies Ringnes, Oskar Sylte, Aass, and Mack.  The recipe was originally Spanish, and brought to the Tønsberg Bryggeri by Torleif Gulliksrud in 1934.  Solo quickly became Norway's most popular soft drink, and until the 1960s was bigger than Coca-Cola in Norway.  In 1999, Pepsi passed Solo in market share, leaving Solo as third most popular.

List of soft drinks by country | This is a list of soft drinks in order of the brand's country of origin.  A soft drink is a beverage that typically contains water (often, but not always, carbonated water), usually a sweetener and usually a flavoring agent.  The sweetener may be sugar, high-fructose corn syrup, fruit juice, sugar substitutes (in the case of diet drinks) or some combination of these.  Soft drinks may also contain caffeine, colorings, preservatives and other ingredients.

Solo (Australian soft drink) | Solo is an Australian lemon-flavoured soft drink manufactured by Schweppes Australia.  Launched in 1968, first in bottle then in a 375ml can by Tarax drinks, it is a ready to drink version of traditional 'pub squash'.  It is the market leader of lemon soft drinks in Australia and grew by over 30% in 2007.

Canada Dry | Canada Dry is a brand of soft drinks owned since 2008 by the American Dr Pepper Snapple Group.  For over a century, Canada Dry has been known for its ginger ale, though the company also manufactures a number of other soft drinks and mixers.  Although Canada Dry originated in Canada, it is now produced in many countries around the globe, including the United States, Mexico, Colombia, the Middle East, Europe and Japan.
Sarsaparilla (soft drink) | Sarsaparilla is a soft drink, originally made from the "Smilax ornata" plant, but now sometimes made with artificial flavours.
Green Spot (soft drink) | Green Spot is a non-carbonated non-caffeinated orange-based soft drink that is sold in Venezuela, Thailand and other Southeast Asian countries.

List of soft drink producers | In every area of the world there are major soft drink producers.  However a few major North American companies are present in most of the countries of the world, such as Pepsi and Coca Cola.
hop2_titles: List of soft drinks by country
response.summary_2: No — they are not produced in the same country. La Croix Sparkling Water originates from G. Heileman Brewing Company in La Crosse, Wisconsin. Solo (Norwegian soft drink) is owned by Norwegian companies Ringnes, Oskar Sylte, Aass, and Mack, and Solo (Australian soft drink) is manufactured by Schweppes Australia.
